aliases: Paneer Tikka with Honey Mustard Marinade

english: Paneer Tikka with Honey Mustard Marinade

ontology.foodex2_description: Paneer (Indian cottage cheese) — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for paneer-based grilled dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for honey mustard paneer tikka; A0FJP (paneer) used as the closest defensible ingredient-level anchor. Preparation facets (grilling, marination) are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to honey mustard paneer tikka as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of honey mustard paneer tikka consists of approximately 6 to 8 cubes of marinated grilled paneer, weighing around 200 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer (Indian cottage cheese), raw — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cottage cheese and mustard marinade components for honey and mustard adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides the base paneer nutrient profile (per 100 g: energy ~265 kcal, protein ~18.3 g, fat ~20.8 g, carbohydrate ~1.2 g). The honey mustard marinade and tandoor preparation add modest carbohydrate from honey (~2–3 g per 100 g of finished dish) and reduce fat slightly through drip loss during grilling. Final per-100 g values are adapted estimates for the finished grilled dish, consistent with ICMR-NIN paneer data and USDA SR Legacy entries for honey (FDC ID 169640) and mustard (FDC ID 172231). Serving-size sodium is elevated by marinade spices and salt.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of dairy foods and high-protein low-carbohydrate foods — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on paneer / Indian cottage cheese and low-carbohydrate grilled protein dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey mustard paneer tikka. The GI of 35 is an analogous estimate derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for paneer and full-fat dairy protein foods (GI range 25–40 for high-fat, high-protein dairy). The honey component contributes modest additional carbohydrate but the overall carbohydrate content per 100 g is low (~6.5 g), keeping the glycemic load per 200 g serving at approximately 4.6 g glucose equivalents, which is low. This estimate is consistent with published GI values for similar high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate grilled Indian cheese dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices and salt (~320 mg per 100 g; ~640 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g restaurant serving of honey mustard paneer tikka contributes approximately 640 mg sodium, representing about 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by health authorities; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~6.5 g per 100 g) with high protein and moderate fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Honey mustard paneer tikka has a low glycemic index of approximately 35 and a low glycemic load per serving (~4.6), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the honey in the marinade adds a small amount of free sugars (~4.2 g per 100 g) and total intake should be monitored within the individual's carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~9.8 g per 100 g) from full-fat paneer and dairy-based marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 200 g serving of honey mustard paneer tikka provides approximately 19.6 g of saturated fat, which exceeds the 20 g daily limit recommended for cardiovascular risk reduction; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and consider lower-fat paneer preparations or reduce frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Mustard Paneer Tikka is a North Indian tandoor-grilled dish made from cubes of fresh homemade paneer (Indian cottage cheese) that are marinated in a spiced mixture of mustard, honey, yogurt, ginger, garlic, turmeric, cumin, coriander, red chilli, lemon juice, and oil before being skewered and cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a dish with a lightly charred, smoky exterior and a soft, creamy interior characteristic of high-quality paneer. The honey provides a subtle sweetness that balances the sharpness of the mustard and the heat of the spices, creating a complex flavour profile that distinguishes this preparation from classic paneer tikka. Nutritionally, honey mustard paneer tikka is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 230 kcal, 16.5 g protein, 16 g fat (of which ~9.8 g is saturated), and only 6.5 g carbohydrate. It is an excellent source of calcium (~480 mg per 100 g) and vitamin B12 (~0.4 mcg per 100 g) from the dairy base. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, the primary concern is saturated fat from full-fat paneer and sodium from the marinade, both of which are relevant for cardiovascular and hypertension management. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible. In Singapore, this dish is served across multiple North Indian restaurants and is popular as a starter or appetiser, often accompanied by mint chutney and sliced onions.
common_user_queries: Is paneer tikka good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for honey mustard paneer tikka exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN paneer base profile with adjustments for honey and mustard marinade components and tandoor cooking. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for high-protein dairy foods. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is methodologically sound and grounded in approved primary sources, but direct measurement data for this specific preparation is not available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN paneer data (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017) and USDA SR Legacy entries for honey and mustard. Direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is not available. 2. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database dairy/protein food entries; no direct GI measurement for honey mustard paneer tikka was found in the literature. 3. Sodium estimate (~320 mg per 100 g) reflects typical restaurant marinade salt levels; actual sodium may vary by restaurant. 4. The dish is served across 7 North Indian restaurants on field survey data (price range SGD 16.90–19.90). 5. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is an Indian dish served in Singapore. 6. FoodEx2 code A0FJP (paneer) is used as the closest ingredient-level anchor; no compound-dish FoodEx2 code for honey mustard paneer tikka exists. 7. No zero-macro justification is required as all core macros are non-zero.
